entry.title: How we could potentially have nestjs as runtime for SAP CAP
entry.content:
Introduction

The motivation for this blog for me was to teach myself nestjs and the stuff that comes with it.

And to make it a little bit more interesting for me, rather than following samples they have, I decided to try replace CAP nodejs runtime (based on express) with nestjs (that also uses express under the hood).

As their docs say, "Nest (NestJS) is a framework for building efficient, scalable Node.js server-side applications. It uses progressive JavaScript, is built with and fully supports TypeScript (yet still enables developers to code in pure JavaScript) and combines elements of OOP (Object Oriented Programming), FP (Functional Programming), and FRP (Functional Reactive Programming)." and also that "Nest provides an out-of-the-box application architecture which allows developers and teams to create highly testable, scalable, loosely coupled, and easily maintainable applications. The architecture is heavily inspired by Angular."

I missed that angular part and got back to it after I researched the rxjs observables in regards to Interceptors, but if you unlike me are familiar with angular already, maybe this whole thing will make more sense to you immediately.

So basically, I was aiming to replace ODataAdapter (with its "middleware") + cds.ApplicationService with something from nestjs ecosystem.

Another major topic for me was transaction handling in CAP nodejs, so I was aiming to take a closer look at what happens there.

From cds perspective I took sample app as a reference model and kept it almost untouched.

TL;DR

The result of the exercise is this repo running in 4 "modes":

  1. og cap mode (npm run cds) - just basic crud for catalog and admin service with single  action implemented to showcase some TX handling
  2. vanilla nodejs mode (npm run node) - replaces ApplicationService with our own vanilla JS class FakeCDSService that does NOT implement auto-txing. Here we still mount full ODataAdapter for both services, but do it ourselves without cds_serve (meaning we instantiate everything ourselves).
  3. nestjs mode (npm run nest) - runs project with nestjs runtime with admin and catalog service having different behaviour:
    1. Admin service has two endpoints: "/odata/v4/admin" served with ODataAdapter + FakeCDSService AND "/rest/v1/admin" served with nestjs + middleware dealing with OData to CQN parsing and serving $metadata and service root endpoints (so that it does act as odata service as well)
    2. Catalog service: we completely drop run-dispatch-handle loop part, and have EmptyCDSService service just as provider for cds model for odata parser for "/rest/v1/catalog" endpoint (still serving odata of course)

Because of this (and the fact that we have a primitive support for $batch) "browse app" is pointed to "/rest/v1/catalog" endpoint while "admin-books app" still uses "/odata/v4/admin"

Modes 2 and 3 use MySQLiteService as a subclass of cap-js/sqlite SQLiteService (aka "new db services" implementation) which has simplified run-dispatch-handle loop and supports auto-txing and manual tx we use for $batch and submitOrder action.

Disclaimer

The code in the repo mentioned above is just PoC intended for purely academic use, please do not use it otherwise.

It barely covers some basic features in terms of CAP functionality: handling of odata requests by a single local service)

Some parts of the code (namely some middleware and multipart handling) are taken from actual cds implementation and therefore belong to SAP.

Also in regards to nestjs there must be of course more and better ways to achieve the same, but author had no prior knowledge in it before starting this project (learning it was part of the project).

CDS (CAP nodejs) part

As already was said, we have two sample cds services: admin and catalog
We removed custom handlers (expect for submitOrder action handler we cover later), so ther is just basic default CRUD logic lest

Expectations regarding the Architecture

With new ODataAdapter that replaces okra odata server in cds8, this is how it was described at recap this year:

  • Adapter instance = express router object used for respective path
    • Router object is an isolated instance of middleware and routes
    • Example: app.use('/odata/v4/catalog', <adapter>)
  • Responsibilities:
    • Early access check (DoS): May the current user use the respective service?
    • Parse the url and, if necessary, the payload
    • Set the transactional boundary (cf. /$batch)
    • Construct and dispatch cds. Request to cds. ApplicationService
    • Send (error) response to client

So, based on this it would be cool if we could have something like this in terms of layers of abstraction:

HTTP LAYER (express app receives incoming http requests and routes it somewhere)

    => ODATA MIDDLEWARE LAYER (produces CQN object)

        => APPLICATION SERVICE (handles CQN object)

            => DB SERVICE (executes CQN objet)

In this case we could just replace APPLICATION SERVICE layer with nestjs runtime to handle CQN for us.

What is important to mention here is that express middleware by definition does this:

  • Execute any code.
  • Make changes to the request and the response objects.
  • End the request-response cycle.
  • Call the next middleware function in the stack.

While express router is a different kind of beast, because it actually handles our http requests.

Why does it matter?

Well, because we would like to have a middleware that is independent from the actual handler - it would just populate some properties of req object for us or serve $metadata and stop early.

But as current implementation of "middleware" is responsible for "Constructing and dispatching cds.Request to cds.ApplicationService", this qualifies it as a "handler" that is tightly coupled to cds.ApplicationService (especially in $batch case) - and so we need to make it a proper middleware ourselves (and we do so).

 

// NOTES:
// - only via srv.run in combination with srv.dispatch inside,
//   we automatically either use a single auto-managed tx for the req (i.e., insert and read after write in same tx)
//   or the auto-managed tx opened for the respective atomicity group, if exists
// - in the then block of .run(), the transaction is committed (i.e., before sending the response) if a single auto-managed tx is used
return service
  .run(() => {
    return service.dispatch(cdsReq).then(result => {
      ... // here could be stuff like _readAfterWrite
      return result
    })
  })
  .then(result => {
    ... // here is actual preparation of result
    res.send(result)
  })
  .catch(err => {
    ... // here is preparation of error
    next(err)
  })

 

ApplicationService and auto-txing + AsyncLocalStorage

As was shown above, our hadler use somthing like this to "execute" the cds.Request we have:

 

service.run( () => { return service.dispatch(cdsReq).then( result => { return result } }).then().catch()

 

What does that mean?

Well, after I spent some time debugging all that srv-api.js and srv-dispatch.js and srv-tx.js stuff, it occured to me that (at least) now in the documentation we have some info regarding what I call "run -> dispatch -> handle loop" covered here AND we have some more details regarding the transactions here.

And to be honest, it made much more sense to me only AFTER I implemented my FakeCDSService, MySQLiteService, and nest stuff and came back to dive deeper into transactions stuff.

So, if my understanding of that logic is correct, those "loops" (calls of run or dispatch with functions as arguments) eventually just make sure we properly set our transactional boundaries for incoming call, and here is where AsyncLocalStorage comes into play:

 

// somewhere in cds-tx.js when we understand we need to set new RootContext
if (typeof fn === 'function') {
  const tx = RootTransaction.for (srv, ctx)
  return cds._context.run(tx, () => Promise.resolve(fn(tx) ).then(tx.commit, tx.rollback))
}

 

And what is does for us, is sets cds.context to be our RootContext with cds.context.tx to be our RootTransaction while all subsequent calls to .run (db, other local or remote services) have access to this context.

You can refer to this article to see example 3 as simplified version of this approach.

But that does not directly start a transaction for us! While it sets transactional boundaries, it actually waits until first "run" or "dispatch".

And this is the tricky part they cover in docs:

 

// The tx object created by srv.tx() and passed to the function fn is a derivate of the service instance, constructed like that:
tx = { __proto__:srv,
  context: { tenant, user, locale }, // defaults from cds.context
  model: cds.model, // could be a tenant-extended variant instead
  commit(){...},
  rollback(){...},
}

 

Basically, first dispatch is replaced with _begin that makes sure that .begin() of DB service is called.

And after that - when all handlers finish their processing - commit or rollback is done.

You can refer to srv/cat-service.js where I cover two almost identical cases for this behavior.

Keep in mind, that indeed once some transaction began, it acquires the connection (from the pool).

But then until it is released, new transaction either needs to wait, or get another connection (if DB supports parallel transctions).

In case of SQLite it can introduce deadlocks (as shown in _submitOrder) OR "freeze" your handlers (imagine a scenario with remote service call that takes some considerable amount of time).

Vanilla nodejs part

So, initially my approach was to remove cds bootstrap process and see how all the stuff runs.

And then try to strip off more and more layers of logic from the cds.ApplicationService.

And if you are interested in this, you can browse commit history in repo, cuz it indeed starts with that.

Eventually, I ended up with having a vanilla js class FakeCDSService which does not have any dependencies to cds runtime.

What that means, is this service does NOT provide our handlers with auto-txing, and all the transaction logic happens at DB level (which makes sense).

And there you can see how does "simple" run -> dispatch -> handle loop looks like.

Of course, this implementation only supports default generic crud handler (kinda ON_READ, ON_UPDATE etc) as we removed before/on/after phases completely.

And basically this is enough to implement simplest working odata service (as I said before, $batch part is hard, so I had to stub some weird stuff there) that uses ODataAdapter.

In our case we actually just implement a subclass of HttpAdapter more or less the same way as original cds implementation does, using their standard handlers.

Nestjs part

Finally, here comes that part with an answer to the original question.

Of course, I had several iterations of code "doing something":

  1. Exactly the same ODataAdapter + middlewares I had for vanilla nodejs, that were used at main.ts. That basically did not change anything.
  2. Then I had (and it still works for AdminService) instance of FakeCDSService that was connected with db service through some nestjs magic, but still used ODataAdapter
  3. And final iteration without instance of FakeCDSService and without ODataAdapter

All three use essentially same MySQLiteService (I just changed the name of the class exposed in provider a couple of times).

So, the 3rd option would be more or less proper answer, meaning:

  1. We need to implement proper middleware that parses urls into proper CQN objects (and in cases of $batch requests we have an array of them in req.batch)
  2. We still need to still register that middleware so that it has access to cds definition of a correponding service to use for parsing
  3. We need to have an instance of simplest EmptyCDSService (we define in cds.provider.ts) so that it stores that definition and has endpoints
  4. And then we need to implement controllers that have db service injected to them via nestjs

For point #2 we can actually replace middeware with Interceptors (like we have in transormations.interceptor.ts to deal with batch multipart body and final formatting of odata response)

Also in that case direct instantiation of this EmptyCDSService in configure(consumer) method can potentially be replaced with some other custom provider (we just need constants like name and endpoint)

And then our controllers (like CatalogService in catalog.module.ts) would be responsible for handling of requests with proper transactionalboundaries.

For example, the way we handle $batch there looks like this (more or less cds'y):

Of course, in reality batch requests can be much more complex with interdependent requests

 

('*batch') // omg $batch just does not work ;(
@UseInterceptors(HandleMultipartInterceptor, AddODataContextInterceptor)
async handleBatch(@Req() req: any, ({ passthrough: true }) res: Response) {
    await (this.dbService as Service).tx( async (tx) => {
        await tx.begin() // this is super important to call for now
        for (const r of req.batch.requests) {
            r.result = await tx.run(r.query)
            r.statusCode = 200
        }
    })
    return
}

 

This actually works for simple non-draft-enabled scenario, so our browse app is able to consume this endpoint.

Notice here, that controller does not deal with Response object at all (compare to our current handlers in cds above)

And of course, same approach with AsyncLocalStorage can be applied, and in fact there is a third party library implementing it for transactions imitating @Transactional annotation in spring.

But that actually brings us to an interesting conclusion (it was unexptected for me at the beginning of this exercise)

As a conclusion

After I researched Transactions handling a little bit more, and then read some stuff about it in regards to nestjs and spring, it occured to me that I was actually reimplementing cap java but with nodejs.

And this was confirmed after I (finally) read cap java docs.

And while of course, with proper framework you can basically re-implement all the existing stuff using the "building blocks" this framework provides you with (like interceptors, providers and that stuff in nest), the question now would be: WHY?

Therefore I am not sure I will put more effort into this "yet another DI strongly typed CAP implementation", but if someone is for whatever reason interested in this topic, please find me on LinkedIn (I am open for projects btw).

 

P.S Thoughts regarding the "new community platform"

I was hesitant to use it, and of course the first thing that I had to deal with was "finding my saved draft".. In short, if you don't remember the board you wanted to post it to, you might be out of luck )

But then there was also this "Your post has been adjusted to remove unsupported HTML found in the message body. The HTML has been updated. Please review the post and send it when you are satisfied." which was a real pain in the ass, cuz I only used wysiwyg editor with simple formatting and code blocks.

In my case,  it looks, the issue was something copied from somewhere, and that introduced divs and spans that this engine could not properly deal with together with code blocks. And I had to manually clean up the html..

Therefore I strongly recommend exporting <html> code of your blog somewhere BEFORE you try pressing "Save Draft"

Overall experience: below average, even worse than previous blog platform, which had some "space for improvement" in regards to content creation tools.

Motivation to write something else: not at all.

Which is rather sad..

entry.title: SAP Developer News, September 19th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/09/19/sap-developer-news-september-19th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

Devtoberfest Launch

  • Devtoberfest Community Home: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/gh-p/Devtoberfest
  • Devtoberfest Events Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Gameboard: https://devrel-tools-prod-scn-badges-srv.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/devtoberfestContest/scnId.Here
  • Devtoberfest Playlist on YouTube: https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d&si=R26dQIns-1e97fU0

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model August 2024 Release

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/aug24

Devtoberfest Crossword Puzzle

  • https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/devtoberfest2024-sap-crossword.html

Transporting content in SAP Build Apps

  •  https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-builders-blog-posts/transporting-content-in-sap-build-apps-sap-cloud-transport-management/ba-p/13737478

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Devtoberfest Launch
2:09 SAP Cloud Application Programming Model August 2024 Release
7:56 Devtoberfest Puzzle
9:06 Transporting content in SAP Build Apps

Transcript

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for September 19th, 2024.

[Sheena] Hello SAP community. The much-awaited Detoberfest 2024 is here, running from September 23rd to October 25th. Devtoberfest helps to prepare for SAP TechEd and offers a mix of educational sessions, hands-on developer tools, and fun activities like Petoberfest, Bugtoberfest, and Spooktoberfest. It is a point-based contest, and the Devtoberfest animated contest game board is already live. You can earn points by attending sessions, completing developer tutorials linked to weekly topics or engaging in activities that benefit the SAP Developer Community.

The grand prize is a retreat at an SAP TechEd Roadshow stop in 2025. Exciting, right? To start participating, join the Devtoberfest group after reviewing the legal terms and conditions posted there.

We will kickstart Devtoberfest on Monday, September 23rd, which is the ABAP/CAP Day. Week 1, we are bringing to you many exciting sessions in ABAP, mainly on clean core extensibility, building partner products on SAP BTP, major updates in embedded analytics, and data integration in ABAP Cloud with SQL services and CDS external entities. Week 1 also covers an exciting session on CAP, it's time to TypeScript (CAP edition).

On tooling Tuesdays, we'll focus on SAP Build Process Automation, Build Code and Build Apps.

Integration Wednesdays will have sessions on FlashPy, CPI Helper, and CPI Lint.

MAD Thursdays will focus on machine learning, AI, and data with sessions on SAP Generative AI Hub, SAP Datasphere, etc.

Frontend Fridays will have sessions on UI5 Freestyle and SAP Fiori tools.

Don't forget to participate in the fun Friday event Petoberfest by posting pictures with your pets at your workstation. So join us in this fun ride of learning and enjoy. Good morning.

[DJ] The latest release of CAP August of 2024, in fact, is out. And with it, there are improvements and fixes to the latest major release 8, which came out earlier this year, in the form of CAP Node.js 8.2.1 and CAP Java 3.2.0. Let me take this opportunity on my walk this morning to go through some of those highlights.

So first up, when the latest major release of CAP came out earlier this year, the new database services went GA, including the database service for SAP HANA. One of the benefits of this new database service for the Node.js runtime is that HANA native associations were no longer required to represent associations at the CDS level. This was great, mostly because it brought longer deploy times when we used SAP HANA Native Associations, plus there was the chance of indirect dependencies also being created. And if you're wondering about the Java runtime, the Java runtime hasn't used native SAP HANA Associations for a while. Anyway, at the time, the release notes advised us to switch off the HANA native associations using configuration. With this August 2024 release, that switch is now automatic. When you start a new project and do, for example, CDS add HANA, you get a switch in the configuration that turns these native associations off by default.

In the area of the CDS language and compiler, you can now use CDS deploy dash dash script to generate a SQL script as a starting point or manual schema migration. Note that in contrast to the existing dash dash dry option, it accepts and produces DDL statements that could lead to data loss. So are A, not supported for automatic schema migration and B, contains relevant warnings in the right places.

In the Node.js runtime, there are also some important changes. One of which is that linked definitions, the class that is used to represent internally structures of services and more, now no longer have the convenience methods such as for each map filter and so on that you would normally expect to have to process the linked definitions as arrays. Because basically linked definitions are not arrays, they're more than that. So these methods have been removed. In my opinion, this is great because it reminds us that we have to treat the data as it is. And so one thing to do is maybe convert the data to arrays using the rest parameter inside, for example, an array, literal array constructor.

Another improvement is that you can now programmatically, i.e. dynamically at runtime, control and activate the CDS debug output at runtime. This means you can turn on debug for cat modules as you go.

In the Java runtime, there's now beta level support for recursive hierarchies with Odata v4 in SAP HANA. Recursive hierarchy is an experimental term in the OData hierarchy vocabulary and is used, for example, with SAP Fiori tree tables. Treetables. You can also now define the name of the Java interfaces generated for CDS entities and structured types using a new annotation. In the multi-tenancy area, activating extensions using the SAP extensibility service can now be done asynchronously. You achieve this by sending a prefer header in the HTTP request with the value of respond async. This in my opinion is a really neat way of coordinating this using the RFC 7240 standard authored by an old acquaintance of mine.

Well I'm almost home but there's just time to tell you about improvements in the tools area. So first for all you IntelliJ users out there the SAP CDS language support plugin is now available on the JetBrains marketplace which means it's directly installable now. Great! For all you TypeScript users there's a new CLI command "CDS TSX", which in contrast to the existing CDS TS command uses a different faster engine. This feature is in beta still at the moment.

So I'm back home. There's one more thing to tell you about and that is if you want to use the SAP Cloud Logging Service instead of the SAP Application Logging Service, you can do that. And there's an option to use telemetry as well, including an option to use Dynatrace. What's not to like? Anyway, that's it for this August 2024 release. See See you next time.

[Shrini and Daniel] Hello, everybody. I'm at Tbilisi, Georgia with Daniel at the EPUB office doing SAP Build CodeJam. It's really great to be here. Yeah, it's been a lot of fun and because it's only a couple days before Devtoberfest, we wanted to tell you something about the contest. So the contest you can earn enough points to get a trip to TechEd perhaps next year and one of the ways to get points is by doing the traditional SAP crossword. So there are topics about SAP Build and JavaScript and even a reference to Analytics Cloud and it would help to know something about Sabrina Carpenter. Can anybody do the Cross Word? Yes, definitely. Of course, it helps to know a little bit about SAP. There are 33 questions. Fairly easy, a few tougher ones, but I think everybody can do it, and it might even be worth a couple thousand points. And it's already published, so even before the Devtoberfest starts, you can accumulate points. So that's pretty good. That's good to know. Yeah. Good luck to everybody.

[Rekha] Hello, everybody. We all know the importance of having development, test, and productive environments during the application development. SAP has recently introduced project versioning for SAP Build apps, web, and mobile projects. This allows you to transport the released project versions between development, testing, and productive subaccounts on SAP BTP. A transport setup of Cloud Transport Management Service, CTMS, Content Agent Service, CAS, and BTP destinations are required to start transporting SAP Build Apps projects between the three tiered dev test prod landscapes. There are four types of subaccounts or tenants involved in the scenario. A source subaccount, this is where the actual development takes place. A target, under this we have two subaccounts, testing, and production, and a central sub-account to administer the content transport between the source and target sub-accounts. In order to enable the transport, you will have to enable the Cloud Foundry, subscribe to SAP Build apps and content agent services, create an instance of content agent, and generate a service key on the source and target sub-accounts.

On the central sub-account, you need to subscribe to Cloud Transport Management service, create an instance of Cloud Transport Management, and generate a service key. The MBS role collection has to be enabled in BTP cockpit to authorize the users for performing the transport activities. SAP Burn Lobby configuration has to be done in the control tower in order to set the sub-account type to decide if it's a dev, test, or prod subaccount. Then BTP destinations has to be set from source to central subaccount, and then from central to target, that is the test and prod subaccounts. Finally, set up the transport landscape using the Cloud Transport Management user interface. For more details on this topic, do refer to this interesting blog post by SAP Build expert Mari Heary. Link is provided in the description box. Enjoy learning and thank you.

entry.title: AD104 - Next steps
entry.content:
In our Teched session AD104 you have learned about how to build CAP applications using SAP Build Code. And how to enrich this application with prebuilt functionality and service integrations by utilizing CAP Plugins.

After entering into the development environment from the SAP Build Code Lobby, we used generative AI by providing it with a textual description of our use case. Joule AI used this input and generated very useful artefacts for us:

  • A complete CDS data model
  • A CDS service definition
  • Fiori Elements UI annotations
  • Sample data

If you think of a typical 3 tier architecture everything is covered. From the CDS data model the CAP framework automatically creates the database statements covering the database layer. The CAP application itself is the server and with our CDS service definition we have defined the behavior of the server and the endpoints it provides (by default via Odata v4). And the UI annotations provide the information to render a Fiori Elements User Interface that matches our model.

So one simple command created a complete full stack application, that we can start up an try out.

And with the sample data (which even fits the business context!) we can try out the application with meaningful test data.

This is a great starting point - and we can start to enhance the application now with additional functionalities and our business logic.

One common requirement across applications is uploading attachments. On our incidents sample app, it would be very helpful to upload supporting documents. Previously, that would have been a challenge connected with a ton of custom code, maintenance and so on. Maybe a thousand lines of code to access low level APIs of an attachment service, where you need to take care of everything. Plus the additional logic - what is the maximum attachment size? How to malware scan it? How to implement the UI to upload and download attachments.

Image instead a single dependency to a reusable package and two lines of code - that includes all of the above already.

That's the power of CAP Plugins and the story behind "Calesi" - CAP level service interfaces.

___

If you want to try out this functionality, the Joule functionalities are now available in SAP Build Code on a trial account.

The instructions for this can be found here:

https://developers.sap.com/mission.sap-build-code-test-drive.html

___

For a list of officially supported CAP plugins, check out the corresponding page in our documentation:

https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/plugins/

For node.js there are two github organizations where all the plugins can be found:

cap-js : https://github.com/search?q=topic%3Aplugin+org%3Acap-js&type=Repositories

cap-js-community: https://github.com/cap-js-community

For a Devtoberfest session, where you can follow along hands on and learn how to add additional plugins and a custom handler to a Joule generated app, see https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4o2Swj89zxU 

___

Any more questions, feedback and so on - feel free to leave a comment!

Thank you for reading 😃

entry.title: Product Updates for SAP Build Code – September 2024 Edition
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

As we wrap up another exciting month, we wanted to take a moment to share some of the latest updates with you. With September coming to a close, we are getting ready for two major events in the SAP Community - Devtoberfest and TechEd. For more information, be sure to read the rest of this release information.

 

Support SAP S/4HANA Cloud Service Integration for Data Model and App Logic

You can use our generative AI assistant Joule in SAP Build Code to help you to write code quickly and easily by providing natural language prompts. As you know, we are constantly listening to your feedback to make our tools even better. One request we got was to enable AI support for external service scenarios. We are excited to tell you that it is now possible to use Joule to generate code for your integrated SAP S/4HANA Cloud services.

You start by creating your full-stack application, importing an external SAP S/4HANA Cloud service, and defining the data model and services. (With Joule, you can easily create your data model and services.)

Data Model and Service creation with the help of Joule

With our new feature, it is now possible to use Joule to enhance the data model you created before: You can add associations between the external entity and local entities, and you can expose the external entity to a local service via a projection.

Using Joule to add an association between the entity Books and the external entity A_BusinessPartner

We've also added support for custom logic generation to read the associated external SAP S/4HANA Cloud entity and to manipulate the exposed external entity.

Using Joule to add a custom logic reading data from the external entity A_BusinessPartner

 

SQL Notebooks for SAP HANA

SAP Build Code offers notebook functionality, similar to the well-known Jupyter notebooks. Since Q3 2024 the SAP HANA tools in SAP Build Code offer a notebook experience for executing SQL statements on an SAP HANA Cloud database.

To get started, create an SAP HANA dev space. Inside the dev space, create a file with the .notebook extension and open it. The file will be opened with the default notebook editor. Next, create a code block. The default code block type should be “SAP HANA Connection Management”. This code block type allows opening, closing, and selecting a database connection. Typing ‘#’ opens the value help with the available commands.

Choose the “List database connections” Command

Choose the “List database connections” command and execute the code block. A list of the available database connections from the SAP HANA Database Explorer view will be shown. You can choose a database connection to use for the notebook by clicking on its name in the list. The selected database will be highlighted with a different background color.

Select Database Connection

Now you can create another code block for executing SQL statements. Be sure to change the code block type to “SAP HANA SQL” by clicking on the “SAP HANA Connection Management” indicator in the bottom right corner and choosing “SAP HANA SQL” from the subsequent dialog.

Execute SQL Queries

Add one or more SQL statements and execute the code block. The results will be shown below the code block.

Add SQL Statements and Execute the Code Block

You can add more code and markdown blocks as needed.

 

Use the Joule functionality to enhance your MDK projects in SAP Build Code

Remember back in June when we announced you could create and manage your mobile development kit (MDK) apps directly from the SAP Build lobby and continue their development using the Storyboard in SAP Build Code? We're thrilled to share another exciting update on that front!

You can now take advantage of the generative AI assistant Joule to enhance your existing MDK projects in SAP Build Code. For further details, I recommend to read this blogpost written by my colleague Jitendra.

 

Devtoberfest and TechEd

Get ready for an exciting journey of learning and collaboration with Devtoberfest and SAP TechEd Virtual!

From September 23rd to October 25th, 2024, join us for Devtoberfest, a celebration of coding and collaboration within the SAP Community. This virtual event is designed to prepare you for the highly anticipated SAP TechEd. My colleague Ravi summarizes all you need to know about SAP Build Code at Devtoberfest. For further information, read the Devtoberfest 2024 Welcome blog post and join the Devtoberfest group in SAP Community to take part in this great event!

And don't forget to mark your calendar for SAP TechEd Virtual on October 8-9, 2024, your gateway to the latest innovations and advancements in SAP technologies! The session catalog is now live, featuring a wide range of topics across various tracks, including the Application Development and Automation and the Data and Analytics tracks, where you'll find dedicated sessions on SAP Build Code. You are looking for a summary where to find SAP Build Code at SAP TechEd? Then I recommend to read this blog post.

Don't miss these opportunities to enhance your technical expertise and connect with the SAP community.

 

Let Us Hear Your Voice!

We prepared a short survey, focused on improving the product to better fit your needs. By participating, you can help us improve SAP Build Code and SAP Business Application Studio. It should only take 5 minutes to complete. Please take the survey now.

 

For further information on new features, please check our roadmaps: roadmap for SAP Build Code, SAPUI5 Road Map. And have a look at What's New in SAP Build Code, the What's New Viewer for SAPUI5 and the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model Release Notes. If you want to stay up-to-date with news, learning resources, and product and strategy updates for SAP Build Code: follow our tag.

We look forward to seeing how you use these new capabilities to create innovative solutions and drive your projects forward. Stay tuned for more updates and happy coding! 😊

entry.title: SAP Developer News, September 26th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast Version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/09/26/sap-developer-news-september-26th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

Extensibility Wizard

  • Blog by Karthik Thirunavukkarasan, overview of Extensibility Wizard: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-extensibility-wizard-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13867427
  • Blog by Seshadri Sreenivas Ramanarayanan, on side-by-side extensions with SAP Build process Automation: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/creating-side-by-side-process-extensions-from-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13867657
  • Documentation:
    • Setup on BTP: https://help.sap.com/docs/build/sap-build-core/use-extensions?version=Cloud
    • Setup on S/4HANA Cloud: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/b2ee5784ccfc45d5acf42d4c60eb5270.html
  • Antonio Maradiaga’s videos on setting up event mesh for SAP Build Process Automation:
    • SAP Tech Bytes: Configuring SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh
    • SAP Tech Bytes: Delivering events from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP Build Process Automation via AEM
  • Devtoberfest Session: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/s-4hana-extensions-start-fast-with-the-new-extensibility-wizard/ev-p/13795541
  • Petoberfest: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-discussions/petoberfest-2024/m-p/13872559

Devtoberfest Week 1 Recap

  • Devtoberfest Homepage: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/gh-p/Devtoberfest
  • Devtoberfest Contest Week 1: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-week-1-contest-activities/ba-p/13872705
  • Devtoberfest Event Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d

Devtoberfest Week 2 Preview

  • Devtoberfest Contest Week 2: Coming Soon
  • Devtoberfest Event Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d

SAP TechEd Community Organized Watch Party

  • SAP TechEd: https://www.sap.com/events/teched.html
  • SAP Community Welcome: https://www.sap.com/events/teched/virtual/flow/sap/te24/catalog/page/catalog/session/1723237330354001PDnG
  • SAP Community Watch Zurich: https://community.sap.com/t5/z%C3%BCrich-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-zurich/ec-p/13873047#M1
  • SAP Community Watch Hamburg: https://community.sap.com/t5/hamburg-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-hamburg/ev-p/13875609
  • SAP Community Watch Sao Paulo: https://community.sap.com/t5/s%C3%A3o-paulo-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-s%C3%A3o-paulo/ec-p/13875927#M3
  • SAP Inside Track Bengaluru: https://community.sap.com/t5/bengaluru-events/announcing-sap-inside-track-bengaluru-5th-october-2024/ev-p/13855659

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Extensibility Wizard
2:09 Devtoberfest Week 1 Recap
4:03 Devtoberfest Week 2 Preview
6:56 SAP TechEd Community Watch Party

Transcript

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for September 26, 2024.

[Daniel and Pepper] For Petoberfest, I'm here with Pepper, and we're here to talk about the Extensibility Wizard, which was released last week and let users create extensions right from within the UI of their SAP app. For the first release, it will work in S/4HANA Cloud and will let you create custom logic, custom fields known as on stack extensions or custom or extension processes known as side-by-side extensions. [Don't go to sleep.]

One of the big benefits is that it's context aware. So for example, if I'm in the sales order application, it will offer me sales order related event triggers to go with my process. There's a little bit of setup involved. So your S/4 HANA Cloud has to know about your SAP BTP and SAP Build installations. That's fairly simple, just involves destinations, trust and communication arrangements. You also have to set up Event Mesh so that your events from S/4 HANA can arrive to process automation.

Luckily, my esteemed colleague Antonio Larediaga has already created two videos to show you very simply how to set this up. One to get your events from S/4 HANA into Event Mesh and then from Event Mesh to Process Automation. The extensibility wizard will undoubtedly be the talk of TechEd and that's why we've set up a Devtoberfest session where Sesh and Kartik can talk at length about how you set this up and how How do you use it? And you will be able to ask them questions. I will put links to all of the videos, the blogs, and the Devtoberfest session in the description.

[Say goodbye.] Bye-bye. 🐶

[Thomas] It seems like just the other day we were starting our planning for this year's Devtoberfest. And here we are, nearly at the end of the first week of the four-week event. My how's time flown. Our team has been super busy both preparing for SAP TechEd virtual, TechEd on tour, as well as delivering these great Devtoberfest sessions to you. At the end of the first week, we've had a really packed week, great attendance. Up over last year, some more people are finding their way to Devtoberfest, which makes us very happy. We've had some excellent sessions across all the topic days.

Keep in mind that Monday is ABAP and CAP day. Tuesday is our day for tooling, Tooling Tuesday, where we're looking at SAP Build, Build Apps, Build Code, Build Process Automation, and how all these tools are bringing innovations for CAP development, low-code, no-code development, and ABAP development as well. Wednesday is our Integration Day. Thursday is MAD, our Machine Learning, AI, and Data Day. And then finally, Friday is both our Front-end Friday, which looks at SAP UI5 and Fiori and other topics related to front-end tools. But Friday is also our Fun Friday, where we have special activities.

Depending upon when you're watching this news item, There may still be time to enter into Petoberfest. Uh, that is our Fun Friday activity for September 27th. Post a picture of your pet in your work environment and, and you can earn some bonus points as well as have fun checking out everybody else's pet pictures as well.

Next Friday, we're going to have another special event. It's going to be our Techtoberfest day on, uh, October the 4th. And on that day, we're going to be asking you to post pictures of your work environment. Show us your laptop set up, your desk set up, whatever cool kind of thing you have set up in your work environment.

Hey, in week two, we will repeat the same daily track structure that you've that you've experienced in this first week. In fact, all four weeks will follow the same track structure, but our Monday is particularly packed with eight individual sessions across CAP and ABAP. We've got some really great things coming in that day. And it's going to be chock full of content. On Tuesday, I'm particularly interested to see the Build Apps, what's coming soon, where they're going to show both new AI innovations and the introduction of SAP UI5 output for Build Apps. Something that I've personally been looking for, for a while. We've got some cool stuff on the integration day around the event architecture and cloud events. We have content on the mad day around generative AI and using CAP with generative AI as well. So a real generative AI kind of day for that particular Thursday. And then finally, for our front end day, we have building elegant applications with Fiori for and Build Code for Android and an iOS Building applications for iOS as well. So we really have a mobile day focused on both Android and iOS

So we've got quite a selection of content for you all throughout the week and keep in mind once we finish week two we'll go directly into TechEd Virtual brought to you live from Germany And then we'll come back with more Devtoberfest, two more weeks of Devtoberfest after TechEd Virtual. And don't forget to enter into the contest. We have activities every day of the week, either by filling out validation tutorials for what you've just saw in your lecture sessions or completing tutorials that go along with them as well. So we've got lots of fun ways for you to earn points towards the big prize that you could win, win, which is, of course, a trip to one of the TechEd in-person events next year. And keep an eye out for TechEd on Tour, where from the ASUG TechConnect event, we're going to be bringing the winners from last year's Devtoberfest. We'll have some great pictures and videos from that event when it takes place in early November. So we look forward to seeing you in all the Devtoberfest and TechEd activities that are coming your way this month.

[Josh] Hey everybody. Are you as excited as I am that it is almost time for SAP TechEd? That's right. October 8th and 9th, SAP TechEd is happening this year. We're going to be coming to you live from Heidelberg, Germany. But if you can't be with us because it's virtual and you still want to talk with others and meet and watch the entire event, you can organize your own watch party. I know that several watch parties have already been organized for the SAP TechEd keynote, which is an hour and a half. It's going to be Michael Amling, Mohammed Alam, several others are going to be putting lots of great information out in this year's keynote. And right after that, for 30 minutes, me and Nora are going to get together and we're going to be hosting a community welcome.

So lots of watch parties are being organized around the world. The SAP community has several websites that I'll put links to in this video so you can see where others are getting together and maybe you can organize your own watch party as well. You can set it up, you can organize it on the SAP community website, and just let others know that you're going to be getting together to watch the Keynote and the Community Welcome and any other parts of SAP TechEd that you want to watch with others. I'll put a link to the one in Zurich, Hamburg, Sao Paulo, and then I think there's going to be one coming from Bengaluru, there's an SAP Inside Track, an SIT event that is happening on October 5th, and if you register for that, I believe you get an invite to the watch party, which is again happening on October 8th, when SAP's TechEd first day is going to go live at 9 a.m. Central European Time. I don't know where you are, so you have to figure out your own times on yourself. That's it, and I can't wait to see you from TechEd.

entry.title: MTA and PNPM combined
entry.content:
When working with a monorepository for your BTP CloudFoundry application, using a package manager like pnpm can be highly beneficial. However, switching to a different package manager requires adapting your build and deployment configurations, specifically in the mta.yaml file for BTP CloudFoundry. With pnpm, each UI component and CAP service are managed as separate packages, unlike a typical CAP application where everything is contained within a single application with various UI components in the App folder. With pnpm, the structure would look more like this:

  • Package folders: the main folder with each UI Component and CAP application (could be more then one).

This structure is a bit different compared to a typical CAP application and requires some changes in the configuration files.

In the mta.yaml file, you'll need to adjust the before-all build commands. Instead of using npm install and cds build, you'll need to replace these with equivalent pnpm commands. Specifically, use pnpm to execute npm install within the CAP package by applying a filter with the pnpm command. Similarly, update the build command to leverage pnpm.

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L174

 

The pnpm build script will execute the build script in the cap package which is in the packages folder:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L28

 

The mta.yaml configuration for the CAP module is normally configured to use “npm” as builder. It is not possible to change this to pnpm. This might cause problems when your CAP services has dependencies to other pnpm packages. Anyway, keep “npm” as builder:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L21

 

The npm build  script of the CAP project (in before-all of mta.yaml) copies the package.json including the devDependencies which cannot be resolved when it contains references to other packages. It cannot resolve the dependencies because the workspace references can only be resolved by pnpm. The default mta build uses npm for the service and cannot be changed to pnpm:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L11

 

As a solution you have to install the npm package  “cds-plugin-ui5” to the CAP app, which will remove the workspace dependencies when building. Special thanks to @pmuessig for this plugin:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/cap/package.json#L9

 

For the UI packages, the mta.yaml configuration needs to be adapted as well. In all html5 modules, the commands “npm install” and “npm run build:cf” have to be replaced with pnpm commands:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/mta.yaml#L58

 

The filter will be used to run the command in the related package. The name in the filter is the name of the package in the package.json:

https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env/blob/aa4d2051b0b82728a889ebaf431ca2b211af1ac0/packages/app/package.json#L2

 

With that, you’re monorepository with multiple packages CAP and UI should be good to go! You can find the full Github repository here: https://github.com/marianfoo/ui5con24-dev-env

entry.title: Use SAP CAP SAPUI5 Smart Filter to Show Value Help that are not Associated Entitites
entry.content:
How to Use SAP CAP and SAPUI5 Smart Filter to Show Value Help for Fields that are not Associated Entities

In cases where you need to allow the user to select values through a Value Help for a specific field that is not related to another table, you can achieve this behavior in the following way:

1. Create a View that Contains the Values for the Value Help

First, you need to create a view that only contains the values you want to display in the Value Help. This view should perform a SELECT on the entity from which you need to retrieve the values and use a GROUP BY to avoid repeating information.

 

@CDS.odata.valuelist
view Modules as
    select from GlobalSetting {
        key GlobalSettingModule
    }
    where
        GlobalSettingModule is not null
    group by
        GlobalSettingModule;

 

2. Add @Common Annotations in the Main View

In the view, entity, or projection that you are using to build the Smart Table, you need to add @Common annotations. These annotations will serve as input for CAP to determine that the previously created view should be used for the Value Help.

 

view ViewGlobalSettingPatterns as
    select from GlobalSetting {
        *,
        @Common.ValueList.CollectionPath: 'Module'
        @Common.ValueList.Parameters    : [{
            $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
            LocalDataProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule',
            ValueListProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule'
        }, {
            $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
            ValueListProperty: 'GlobalSettingModule'
        }]
        GlobalSettingModule
    }
    where
        GlobalSettingClassification = 'PATTERN';

 

3. Expose the Views in the Service

You need to expose the new views so that the service can access them and use them as a data source.

 

service MasterDataService {
    entity GlobalSetting             as projection on model.GlobalSetting;
    @readonly
    entity ViewGlobalSettingPatterns as projection on model.ViewGlobalSettingPatterns;
    @readonly
    @CDS.odata.valuelist
    entity Type                      as projection on model.Types;
    @readonly
    entity Module                    as projection on model.Modules;
}

 

4. Verify the OData Metadata

It is important to verify that the metadata correctly shows the annotations. The CollectionPath is the key point since it tells CAP which resource will be used to display the data in the Value Help.

The metadata should look something like this:

 

<Annotations xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Target="MasterDataService.ViewGlobalSettingPatterns/GlobalSettingModule">
    <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
        <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
            <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Module"/>
            <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                <Collection>
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                        <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                    </Record>
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="GlobalSettingModule"/>
                    </Record>
                </Collection>
            </PropertyValue>
        </Record>
    </Annotation>
</Annotations>

 

5. Configure the Filter in the Smart Component

Finally, you just need to configure the Smart Filter, where you specify which field will display the Value Help.

 

<smartFilterBar:ControlConfiguration
    key="GlobalSettingModule"
    label="Module"
    visibleInAdvancedArea="true"
    hasValueHelpDialog="true"/>

 

 

Result

Anthony Ramos Romero.

entry.title: Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account
entry.content:
How to Configure an SAP CAP Application to Connect to an SAP HANA Cloud Database in Another Global Account or Subaccount Without HDI

When your SAP HANA Cloud database is in another Global Account or subaccount, which prevents the natural use of the HDI container for database object management, you need to follow the steps below to configure your SAP CAP application and achieve a successful connection.

1: Configure a User-Provided Service

It is essential to create a user-provided service using the Cloud Foundry command line. This is important because SAP CAP requires the service to have three specific tags to automatically determine which configuration to use.

Run the following command:

cf create-user-provided-service ups-database-external -t "hana,database,relational"
  • ups-database-external: This is the name you assign to your service.
  • -t "hana,database,relational": These are the tags that CAP uses to identify the database service.

2: Modify the Service's JSON in SAP BTP

After creating the service via the command line, you need to specify the connection data in the service's JSON in SAP BTP.

The JSON should contain the following:

{
  "encrypt": true,
  "host": "your-hana-cloud-host",
  "password": "your-password",
  "port": "443",
  "schema": "YOUR_SCHEMA_NAME",
  "sslValidateCertificate": true,
  "user": "your-username"
}
  • host: The address of your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • user and password: The credentials of the user with permissions to access the database schema.
  • schema: The schema where your database objects are located or will be created.
  • encrypt and sslValidateCertificate: Settings to ensure the connection is secure via SSL/TLS.
  • port: The connection port, generally "443" for secure connections.

3: Modify package.json or .cdsrc.json

You need to tell SAP CAP which service to use to obtain the credentials. This is done by modifying your project's package.json or .cdsrc.json file.

Add or update the "requires" section as follows:

{
  "requires": {
    "db": {
      "kind": "hana",
      "credentials": {
        "service": "ups-database-external"
      }
    },
    "[production]": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "db": {
        "kind": "hana",
        "credentials": {
          "service": "ups-database-external"
        }
      },
      "data": false
    }
  }
}
  • "service": "ups-database-external": Indicates to CAP that it should use the provided service named ups-database-external.
  • "[production]": Ensures that the same configuration is used in the production profile.

4: Remove HDI-Related Configurations

Since you are not using HDI for database object management, you need to remove all references and configurations related to HDI in your mta.yaml file or your project's deployment configurations.

This includes:

  • Removing modules or resources that create or manage HDI containers.
  • Deleting dependencies between modules and HDI resources.

5: Bind the Service to the CAP Application Instance in SAP BTP

For your CAP application to take the specified credentials and configuration, you need to bind the provided service to your application's instance in SAP BTP.

You can do this in two ways:

Option 1: Manual Binding

After deploying the application, run the command:

cf bind-service your-application-name ubs-database-external

Then, restart your application:

cf restart your-application-name

Option 2: Automatic Binding in mta.yaml

Add the binding in your mta.yaml file so that it is performed automatically during deployment.

Example of how to define the binding in mta.yaml:

modules:
  - name: your-application-name
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    requires:
      - name: ups-database-external
resources:
  - name: ups-database-external
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: user-provided
      service-name: ups-database-external

6: Manually Deploy the Database Objects

Since you are not using HDI, you need to manually deploy the database objects in your SAP HANA Cloud instance.

1. Generate the SQL Scripts

From your CDS models, generate the SQL scripts using the following commands:

cds compile db/ --to sql --src db cds compile srv/ --to sql --src srv

These commands generate SQL scripts from your model definitions in the db/ and srv/ directories.

2. Execute the Scripts in the Database Console

  • Access SAP HANA Database Explorer.
  • Connect to your SAP HANA Cloud instance.
  • Execute the generated SQL scripts to create the necessary tables and views in your schema.

Anthony Ramos Romero
